enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hostable app for sending client-side encrypted, self-destructing notes and files.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Enclosed is a minimalistic web app for sending private and secure notes. Content is encrypted client-side with AES-GCM using a 256-bit key derived via PBKDF2, so the server never has access to the plaintext of a note or its attached files.

Notes can be protected with a password, given an expiration time, or set to self-destruct after being read once. Enclosed is open source under the Apache 2.0 license and can be self-hosted with a single Docker command, in addition to the hosted instance at enclosed.cc.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Developers and privacy-conscious users who need a quick, disposable way to share credentials, API keys, or sensitive text with someone else without leaving a permanent, server-readable copy behind.

features.title: Client-side AES-GCM encryption
features.description: Notes are encrypted in the browser before upload using a 256-bit key derived with PBKDF2, so the server stores only ciphertext.

features.title: Read-once self-destruction
features.description: Notes can be configured to delete themselves automatically after being opened a single time.

features.title: Expiration timers
features.description: Set a note to automatically expire and become inaccessible after a chosen period.

features.title: Password protection
features.description: Add an optional password requirement on top of the encrypted link for an extra layer of access control.

features.title: File attachments
features.description: Attach files to a note, encrypted the same way as the note text itself.

features.title: Command-line interface
features.description: Create and manage notes directly from the terminal instead of the web UI.

features.title: Self-hosting via Docker
features.description: Run your own private instance with a single Docker container rather than relying on the public enclosed.cc site.

pros: True zero-knowledge design — the server never sees unencrypted note content
Open source (Apache 2.0) with a self-hostable Docker image
Free hosted instance available with no signup required
Read-once and expiration options limit exposure of shared secrets
CLI support for scripting note creation
cons: No native mobile apps, only a responsive web interface
No documented public REST API for third-party integrations
Built for short text/small file sharing, not large file transfer

faqs.question: Is Enclosed free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. The hosted instance at enclosed.cc is free, and the software is open source and free to self-host.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Enclosed?
faqs.answer: Yes, it can be deployed with a single Docker command, with configuration and troubleshooting guides available in the official docs.

faqs.question: Does Enclosed store my note content?
faqs.answer: No. Notes are encrypted client-side before they reach the server, so Enclosed's operators cannot read note contents.

faqs.question: What happens after a note is read?
faqs.answer: You can configure a note to self-destruct after its first view, or set a fixed expiration time instead.

use_cases.title: Sharing credentials with a teammate
use_cases.description: Send a password, token, or API key that automatically disappears after being viewed once.

use_cases.title: Sending sensitive documents privately
use_cases.description: Attach a file to an encrypted, expiring note instead of emailing it as a plain attachment.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Enclosed is an open-source, self-hostable app for sending client-side encrypted, self-destructing private notes and files.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source Go client/server for sharing files from the terminal with server-side encryption, MIT licensed.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: goploader is a lightweight, open-source file-sharing tool built to make it easy to share files straight from the terminal. It consists of a Go client and a Go server that communicate over plain multipart HTTP form uploads, which means files can also be pushed with curl, httpie, or wget without any special client software.

Files are encrypted with AES as they're received and streamed through encryption without being fully loaded into memory; the decryption key is never stored on the server. Licensed under MIT, it's free to run against the maintainer's hosted instance or to self-host on your own server behind HTTPS via Caddy and Let's Encrypt.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Developers and sysadmins who want a minimal, scriptable way to upload and share files from the command line without a web dashboard or account system.

features.title: Curl/httpie/wget-compatible uploads
features.description: Files can be uploaded with standard command-line HTTP clients instead of a proprietary client library.

features.title: Server-side AES encryption
features.description: Uploaded files are encrypted upon receipt, with the decryption key never persisted on the server.

features.title: Streamed encryption
features.description: Files are encrypted and served as a stream rather than being buffered fully in memory.

features.title: HTTPS by default
features.description: The reference server setup uses Caddy with Let's Encrypt for automatic HTTPS.

features.title: Go client and server binaries
features.description: Both components are written in Go with minimal dependencies for easy cross-platform builds.

features.title: Self-hostable, MIT licensed
features.description: Full source and build instructions are available for running your own private instance.

pros: Extremely lightweight with minimal dependencies
Free and open source under the MIT license
Encryption key is never stored on the server
Works with standard command-line HTTP tools, not just a proprietary client
cons: No web dashboard beyond a basic file-serving page
Requires command-line comfort to build and operate
Smaller-scale project with a narrower feature set than full file-sharing platforms

faqs.question: Is goploader free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's free and open source under the MIT license, whether you use the maintainer's instance or run your own.

faqs.question: Can I self-host goploader?
faqs.answer: Yes. Build instructions and server configuration are documented for deploying your own server component.

faqs.question: Do I need a special client to upload files?
faqs.answer: No. Uploads work with standard tools like curl, httpie, or wget since the server accepts plain multipart form uploads.

faqs.question: How are files protected?
faqs.answer: Files are AES-encrypted on receipt and streamed through decryption only when served; the decryption key is never stored server-side.

use_cases.title: Quick terminal file sharing
use_cases.description: Push a file to a shareable link directly from a shell session or script.

use_cases.title: Private lightweight file host
use_cases.description: Run a personal or team file-drop server without adopting a heavier full-featured platform.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: goploader is an open-source Go tool for sharing files from the terminal, with server-side AES encryption and curl-compatible uploads.

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted, open-source image CDN with on-the-fly resizing, filters, and album creation.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: PictShare is a self-hostable content delivery tool for uploading and serving images. Images can be uploaded as a file, via a remote URL, or as Base64-encoded content, and returned as a shareable link such as pictshare.net/abcef123.jpg.

Beyond static hosting, PictShare supports on-the-fly modifications through URL parameters — resizing, aspect-ratio changes, and filters like grayscale — plus the ability to group multiple images into immutable albums, without permanently altering the original upload.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Site owners, forum operators, and developers who want a private, self-hosted image host with a simple upload API instead of relying on third-party image CDNs.

features.title: Multi-source upload
features.description: Accepts images uploaded as a file, a remote URL, or Base64-encoded data.

features.title: URL-based image transforms
features.description: Resize, crop, and adjust images on the fly by appending parameters to the image URL, without editing the original file.

features.title: Filters
features.description: Apply effects such as grayscale directly through URL modifiers.

features.title: Album creation
features.description: Group multiple uploaded images into a shareable, immutable album.

features.title: Delete codes
features.description: Each upload gets a code that lets the uploader remove the content later.

features.title: Self-hostable
features.description: Deployable on your own server rather than depending on the public pictshare.net instance.

pros: Simple, curl-friendly upload workflow
Flexible on-the-fly image resizing and filtering via URL parameters
Free and self-hosted, keeping images off third-party services
Lightweight compared to full media-management platforms
cons: Focused on images only, not general file types
Documentation is thin compared to larger self-hosted projects
No mobile app; access is via the web/API only

faqs.question: Is PictShare free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's free, open-source software that can be self-hosted at no cost.

faqs.question: Can I self-host PictShare?
faqs.answer: Yes, the source is available on GitHub and it can be deployed on your own server, including via Docker.

faqs.question: How do I resize an image?
faqs.answer: Append size parameters to the returned image URL, such as an 800x600 forced-size modifier, to get a transformed version.

faqs.question: Does PictShare support video?
faqs.answer: PictShare is documented and designed specifically for image hosting.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted image CDN
use_cases.description: Host images for a blog, forum, or app without relying on a third-party image service.

use_cases.title: Screenshot/upload target
use_cases.description: Use as a private endpoint for tools that upload images and need a shareable link back.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: PictShare is a free, self-hosted image CDN with on-the-fly resizing, filters, and album creation for developers and site owners.

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted, open-source platform for securely sharing files with clients, with access control and audit logs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ProjectSend is a self-hosted file-sharing platform built for professionals who need to distribute files to clients while keeping full control over access and privacy. It's released under the GNU GPL v2 license and is run entirely on infrastructure you control, so you retain full ownership of your data.

It offers client-specific access controls with detailed audit logging, two-factor authentication (email codes or TOTP apps), AES-256-GCM server-side file encryption, download limits, disk quota management, LDAP/Active Directory integration, and support for AWS S3 or other external storage. Clients can also be permitted to upload files back to their own account.

The project reports over 100,000 active installations and community translations covering more than 70 languages. It requires PHP 8.2+, MySQL 5.0+, and Apache or Nginx, and can be deployed via Docker or on shared hosting.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Agencies, freelancers, and firms (e.g., legal, accounting, creative) that need an auditable, client-facing file exchange instead of emailing attachments or using a generic cloud drive.

features.title: Client-specific access controls
features.description: Restrict which files each client or client group can see and download, with detailed activity logs.

features.title: Two-factor authentication
features.description: Supports email-code and TOTP-app based 2FA for user accounts.

features.title: AES-256-GCM file encryption
features.description: Files are encrypted server-side using AES-256-GCM.

features.title: Download limits and disk quotas
features.description: Cap how many times a file can be downloaded and set per-user or per-client storage quotas.

features.title: External storage support
features.description: Store files on Amazon S3 or other supported external storage instead of local disk.

features.title: Client uploads
features.description: Allow clients to upload files back to their own account when enabled.

features.title: LDAP/Active Directory integration
features.description: Authenticate users against an existing LDAP or AD directory.

features.title: Automatic file expiration
features.description: Configure files to automatically stop being accessible after a set period.

pros: Mature project with a large installed base (100,000+ reported installations)
Free and open source under GPL v2, with full data ownership when self-hosted
Granular per-client access controls and audit logging
Enterprise-friendly features such as LDAP/AD and 2FA
Translated into 70+ languages by the community
cons: Requires managing your own PHP/MySQL/Apache-or-Nginx hosting stack
Admin-focused interface is geared toward file managers rather than end consumers
No documented public REST API for automated integrations

faqs.question: Is ProjectSend free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's free and open source under GPL v2, with optional Patreon support for the developers.

faqs.question: What are the hosting requirements?
faqs.answer: PHP 8.2+, MySQL 5.0+, and Apache2 or Nginx, with support for shared hosting and Docker deployment.

faqs.question: Can clients upload files?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the administrator enables client uploads, clients can upload files to their own account.

faqs.question: Does it support two-factor authentication?
faqs.answer: Yes, via email codes or TOTP authenticator apps.

faqs.question: Can I use S3 for storage?
faqs.answer: Yes, ProjectSend supports AWS S3 and other external storage in addition to local disk.

use_cases.title: Client deliverable handoff
use_cases.description: Give each client a private area to receive and download completed work with a full audit trail.

use_cases.title: Regulated document exchange
use_cases.description: Share sensitive files with encryption, 2FA, and download limits for compliance-conscious workflows.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: ProjectSend is a free, self-hosted, open-source platform for sharing files with clients, with access controls and audit logging.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted file sharing tool with resumable uploads/downloads for Linux, Mac, and Windows.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Quickshare (marketed as QuickShare) is a simple, self-hosted file sharing tool designed to let users exchange files across devices without relying on a third-party cloud provider. It's open source under the LGPL-3.0 license and runs cross-platform on Linux, Mac, and Windows.

It supports resumable uploads and downloads, bulk file transfers, and streaming uploads, along with directory sharing for both registered and anonymous users. User management includes role-based access, per-user speed limiting, and storage quotas, and files can be accessed via a web browser or QR code scanning.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Individuals and small teams who want a lightweight, self-hosted alternative to commercial cloud storage for sharing and syncing files across devices.

features.title: Resumable uploads and downloads
features.description: Interrupted transfers can pick back up instead of restarting from scratch.

features.title: Bulk and streaming transfers
features.description: Supports uploading multiple files at once and streaming uploads.

features.title: QR code access
features.description: Scan a QR code to quickly open a shared file or folder from a mobile device.

features.title: Directory sharing
features.description: Share whole directories with registered or anonymous users.

features.title: Role-based user management
features.description: Manage users with defined roles, per-user speed limits, and storage quotas.

features.title: Cross-platform
features.description: Runs on Linux, Mac, and Windows.

pros: Free and open source under LGPL-3.0
Resumable transfers handle unstable connections well
Runs natively across Linux, Mac, and Windows
Lightweight compared to full cloud-storage suites
cons: Smaller community than mainstream self-hosted alternatives like Nextcloud
No documented public REST API for third-party integrations
Interface is basic rather than feature-rich

faqs.question: Is Quickshare free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's free and open source under the LGPL-3.0 license.

faqs.question: What platforms are supported?
faqs.answer: Quickshare runs on Linux, Mac, and Windows.

faqs.question: Can anonymous users share files?
faqs.answer: Yes, directory sharing can be configured for both registered and anonymous users.

faqs.question: Is there a demo?
faqs.answer: A hosted demo has been made available for testing before self-hosting.

use_cases.title: Personal cloud alternative
use_cases.description: Store and access your own files from any device without a third-party provider.

use_cases.title: Team file exchange over unstable networks
use_cases.description: Use resumable uploads/downloads to reliably move large files.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: QuickShare is a free, open-source, self-hosted file sharing tool with resumable uploads for Linux, Mac, and Windows.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted file-sharing platform where uploads/downloads bypass the server via presigned URLs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Safebucket is an open-source, on-premise file-sharing platform whose defining design choice is that files never pass through the application server. Uploads and downloads go directly between clients and your storage backend using cryptographically signed, time-limited presigned URLs, while the server only handles metadata, access control, and audit logging.

It's SSO-first, authenticating users through OIDC against your existing identity provider rather than managing separate passwords. Sharing happens through buckets with explicit membership and role-based permissions (owner, contributor, viewer), and quick/reverse share links can be protected with a password, a maximum download count, or a maximum view count.

The stack is a Go REST API paired with a React web interface, PostgreSQL or SQLite for the database, and support for S3-compatible storage such as AWS S3 or self-hosted MinIO — with storage, database, events, cache, and notification components all designed to be swappable.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Engineering and IT teams that want an internal, S3-backed file-sharing service with SSO-only authentication and fine-grained, bucket-level access control.

features.title: Presigned-URL transfers
features.description: Files move directly between clients and your storage backend, bypassing the application server entirely.

features.title: SSO-first authentication
features.description: Authenticate users via OIDC against your existing identity provider instead of managing separate passwords.

features.title: Bucket-scoped, role-based access
features.description: Sharing is organized around buckets with owner, contributor, and viewer roles.

features.title: Swappable infrastructure
features.description: Storage, database, event, cache, and notification components can each be swapped for alternatives such as S3, MinIO, or GCS.

features.title: Quick/reverse share links
features.description: Generate public share links protected by password, maximum downloads, or maximum views.

features.title: Audit logging
features.description: Structured logs track access and activity across buckets.

pros: Files bypass the app server via presigned URLs, reducing server load and exposure
SSO-first design avoids managing separate user passwords
Modular architecture lets you swap storage, database, and other components
Open source and self-hostable on your own infrastructure
cons: Newer project with a smaller track record than established file-sharing platforms
Requires configuring an OIDC identity provider and S3-compatible storage
Documentation and ecosystem are still maturing

faqs.question: Is Safebucket free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's open-source and free to self-host.

faqs.question: What storage backends are supported?
faqs.answer: S3-compatible storage such as AWS S3 or self-hosted MinIO, among other swappable options.

faqs.question: Does it require an identity provider?
faqs.answer: Safebucket is SSO-first, designed to authenticate through OIDC against an existing identity provider.

faqs.question: Do files pass through the Safebucket server?
faqs.answer: No, uploads and downloads use presigned URLs so files transfer directly between the client and storage backend.

use_cases.title: Internal S3-backed file portal
use_cases.description: Give a team a private, self-hosted file-sharing service backed by your own object storage.

use_cases.title: SSO-gated file access
use_cases.description: Restrict file sharing to users authenticated through your organization's identity provider.
use_cases.audience: Organizations with an existing OIDC provider

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Safebucket is an open-source, self-hosted file-sharing platform where files bypass the server via presigned URLs and SSO-first auth.

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted, open-source image sharing platform with password protection, expiring links, and ShareX integration.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Slink is a self-hosted image sharing platform built with Symfony and SvelteKit that lets you run your own private image host instead of relying on third-party services. All shares are private by default, accessible only to whoever holds the link, with optional password protection and expiring shares that auto-revoke after a chosen time.

It integrates with ShareX for automatic screenshot uploads, offers hierarchical tag management with search, and supports local, SMB, or AWS S3 storage. Authentication covers email/password sign-in as well as SSO via Google, Authentik, Keycloak, Authelia, Pocket ID, or a custom OIDC provider, alongside personal API keys and Docker-based deployment.
Slink is open source under the AGPL-3.0 license, with the primary repository holding several hundred GitHub stars.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Individuals and teams who capture and share a lot of screenshots or images and want a private, SSO-capable image host instead of a public third-party service.

features.title: Private-by-default sharing
features.description: Images are accessible only via their link unless additional protections are added.

features.title: Password protection and expiration
features.description: Shares can require a password and auto-revoke after a set time.

features.title: ShareX integration
features.description: Configure ShareX or similar capture tools to upload screenshots directly to your Slink instance.

features.title: Tag management and search
features.description: Organize images with hierarchical tags and locate them through search.

features.title: Multiple storage backends
features.description: Store images locally, on SMB shares, or in AWS S3.

features.title: SSO authentication
features.description: Sign in via Google, Authentik, Keycloak, Authelia, Pocket ID, or a custom OIDC provider.

features.title: Admin tools
features.description: Manage users, moderate images, and review storage analytics from an admin panel.

features.title: Personal API keys
features.description: Generate API keys for programmatic uploads and integrations.

pros: Strong privacy defaults with password-protected and expiring shares
Broad SSO provider support for team deployments
Multiple storage backend options including S3
Active open-source project with several hundred GitHub stars
cons: Focused on image hosting rather than general file sharing
AGPL-3.0 license carries copyleft obligations for modified redistributions
Requires familiarity with the Symfony/SvelteKit stack to customize

faqs.question: Is Slink free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's free and open source under the AGPL-3.0 license.

faqs.question: Does Slink integrate with ShareX?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports ShareX and similar capture clients for automatic screenshot uploads.

faqs.question: Can I use S3 for storage?
faqs.answer: Yes, alongside local disk and SMB storage options.

faqs.question: Does Slink support single sign-on?
faqs.answer: Yes, including Google, Authentik, Keycloak, Authelia, Pocket ID, and custom OIDC providers.

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted file and media sharing server built for ShareX-style capture tools, with a REST API.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: XBackBone is a self-hosted platform for sharing files and media, built to act as a backend for capture tools like ShareX so that uploads are turned into short, shareable links. It supports images, video, audio, PDFs, and code snippets, and can store files locally or on Amazon S3, FTP, or SFTP.

The platform de-duplicates content through fingerprinting, allows private or public uploads with optional password protection and expiration, and exposes a REST API with token authentication for automation. User accounts support two-factor authentication and WebAuthn, and admins can manage users and disk quotas.
XBackBone is released under the Apache 2.0 license and free to self-host. According to its documentation, version 4.x is under active development while version 3.x remains supported.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Individuals and teams who use ShareX or similar capture tools and want a private, self-hosted destination for screenshots, video clips, and other files, with API access for automation.

features.title: ShareX and capture-client integration
features.description: Configure capture tools to upload directly to your XBackBone instance and get back a short link.

features.title: Multi-file-type support
features.description: Handles images, video, audio, PDFs, and code, not just screenshots.

features.title: Multiple storage backends
features.description: Store uploads on local disk, Amazon S3, FTP, or SFTP.

features.title: Content de-duplication
features.description: Fingerprinting avoids storing duplicate copies of the same file.

features.title: Password protection and expiration
features.description: Uploads can be private or public, with optional passwords and expiration settings.

features.title: REST API with token authentication
features.description: Automate uploads and management through a documented API.

features.title: Two-factor authentication and WebAuthn
features.description: Secure user accounts beyond a simple password.

features.title: Admin controls
features.description: Manage users and enforce disk quotas from an admin panel.

pros: Purpose-built for ShareX and similar capture-tool workflows
Flexible storage backend options including S3, FTP, and SFTP
REST API enables automation and custom integrations
Free and open source under Apache 2.0
cons: Version 4.x was still under active development per the docs, with 3.x kept as the supported line, so a migration path should be checked before upgrading
Requires self-hosting setup and ongoing server maintenance
Interface is aimed at technical users rather than casual consumers

faqs.question: Is XBackBone free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's free and open source under the Apache 2.0 license.

faqs.question: Does it work with ShareX?
faqs.answer: Yes, XBackBone is designed to act as a backend for ShareX and similar capture clients.

faqs.question: What storage backends are supported?
faqs.answer: Local disk, Amazon S3, FTP, and SFTP.

faqs.question: Is there an API?
faqs.answer: Yes, a REST API with token authentication is available for automating uploads and management.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source CSS theme that restyles Apache's mod_autoindex directory listings.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Apaxy is a customizable theme that restyles the directory listings Apache generates via its mod_autoindex module, replacing the default bland listing with CSS-based styling. It's a drop-in set of static assets rather than an application — there's no server or database to run.

It supports custom MIME-type icons through .htaccess editing, optional jQuery-based enhancements, and custom welcome messages or instructions per directory. Apaxy only changes appearance; it does not restructure the underlying HTML table that Apache generates. It's open source, hosted on GitHub, and created by Adam Whitcroft.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Sysadmins and homelab users who expose Apache's built-in directory listings for file access and want a nicer, more branded look without adopting a full file-sharing application.

features.title: CSS-based restyling
features.description: Overlays custom styling on Apache's default mod_autoindex directory listing.

features.title: Custom MIME-type icons
features.description: Assign specific icons to file types by editing .htaccess.

features.title: Optional jQuery enhancements
features.description: Add JavaScript-based interactivity on top of the static listing.

features.title: Custom welcome messages
features.description: Show custom instructions or messages at the top of a directory listing.

features.title: Drop-in installation
features.description: No application, database, or server process to run — just static CSS/JS/icon assets.

pros: Free and extremely lightweight — just static assets
No application logic or database to maintain or secure
Easy to drop into an existing Apache directory-listing setup
Open source and available on GitHub
cons: Only changes appearance, not the underlying HTML table Apache generates
Requires Apache with mod_autoindex specifically; not usable with Nginx or other servers
Not a file-sharing application — no accounts, share links, or expiration

faqs.question: Does Apaxy require an application or database?
faqs.answer: No, it's purely CSS, JavaScript, and icon assets layered on top of Apache's directory listing.

faqs.question: Does it work with Nginx?
faqs.answer: No, it's specifically built for Apache's mod_autoindex module.

faqs.question: Is Apaxy free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's free and open source.

faqs.question: Who maintains Apaxy?
faqs.answer: It was created by Adam Whitcroft and is hosted on GitHub.

use_cases.title: Nicer-looking exposed file directories
use_cases.description: Give an Apache-served directory listing a modern look without building a file-sharing app.

use_cases.title: Personal or homelab file archives
use_cases.description: Style a simple static file archive served directly by Apache.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Apaxy is a free, open-source CSS theme that restyles Apache's mod_autoindex directory listings for a cleaner look.

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted personal cloud storage and media manager that stores metadata in sidecar files instead of a database.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ClyoCloud is a self-hosted personal cloud storage and media management application designed as a privacy-focused alternative to mainstream cloud services, letting you store, organize, stream, and share files from your own hardware. Instead of requiring a database engine like SQLite, PostgreSQL, or MongoDB, it stores metadata in .cloudinfo sidecar files placed directly alongside each file.

It's built on Nuxt 3 (Vue 3) with a Nitro backend, styled with Tailwind CSS, and uses ffmpeg for media processing. Features include TOTP-based two-factor authentication, favorites and tag-based collections, activity logs for file operations, automatic thumbnail generation, range-aware video streaming, and public sharing links.
The project is developed and hosted by a single maintainer (WeeXnes) on a self-hosted Gitea instance rather than a mainstream platform like GitHub.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Individuals and homelab users who want a lightweight personal cloud without setting up a separate database, and who value media features like thumbnailing and streaming.

features.title: Database-free metadata storage
features.description: Metadata is stored in .cloudinfo sidecar files next to each resource instead of a separate database engine.

features.title: Two-factor authentication
features.description: TOTP-based 2FA (e.g., Google Authenticator, Authy) protects the dashboard.

features.title: Favorites and collections
features.description: Star important files for quick access or organize them dynamically with tags.

features.title: Activity logs
features.description: Detailed logs capture file additions, moves, and deletions.

features.title: Automatic thumbnail generation
features.description: Media files get thumbnails generated automatically for browsing.

features.title: Range-aware video streaming
features.description: Stream video with proper range-request support rather than requiring a full download first.

features.title: Public sharing links
features.description: Generate links to share individual files publicly.

pros: No database dependency simplifies backup and migration — metadata travels with the files
Built on a modern Nuxt 3 / Vue 3 stack
Media-focused features like streaming and automatic thumbnails
Fully self-hosted, keeping data on your own hardware
cons: Personal/indie project maintained by a single developer with a limited track record
Smaller feature set than established platforms like Nextcloud
Source is hosted on a personal Gitea instance rather than a mainstream platform, and documentation is minimal

faqs.question: Is ClyoCloud free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's a free, self-hosted solution.

faqs.question: Does it require a database?
faqs.answer: No, it stores metadata in sidecar .cloudinfo files next to each file instead of using a database engine.

faqs.question: Does it support video streaming?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports range-aware video playback for streaming media.

faqs.question: Can I share files publicly?
faqs.answer: Yes, ClyoCloud supports generating public sharing links.

faqs.question: Where is the source code hosted?
faqs.answer: On a self-hosted Gitea instance at code.weexnes.dev, run by the project's maintainer.

use_cases.title: Lightweight personal cloud
use_cases.description: Run a private cloud storage instance without provisioning a separate database.

use_cases.title: Home media library
use_cases.description: Organize, thumbnail, and stream personal media from self-hosted infrastructure.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: ClyoCloud is a free, self-hosted personal cloud storage and media manager that stores metadata in sidecar files, no database needed.
